name: NuGet Gallery API
what_it_does: The NuGet Gallery API v3 provides a modern, protocol-based interface to the official .NET package registry. Agents can query a service index to discover endpoint URLs, search packages, retrieve package metadata and version history, download .nupkg files, and push or delete packages. The v3 API is JSON-LD based and highly cacheable.
use_cases: Resolving NuGet package versions and metadata for automated csproj or packages.config updates
Searching NuGet.org for .NET libraries to satisfy a dependency requirement
Fetching package dependency manifests for transitive dependency analysis
Checking for newer versions of packages in a .NET project for automated update PRs
Publishing NuGet packages to NuGet.org as part of a CI/CD release pipeline
not_for: Private package hosting without Azure Artifacts or a self-hosted NuGet server
Actual package restoration into a .NET project (use dotnet CLI or MSBuild)
best_when: An agent needs to resolve, search, or publish .NET packages from NuGet.org as part of a build automation or dependency analysis workflow.
avoid_when: You need private package feeds (use Azure Artifacts, GitHub Packages, or a self-hosted NuGet server), or you need to restore packages into a project directory.

auth.notes: Read operations (search, metadata, download) are unauthenticated. Package push and delete require an API key passed via the X-NuGet-ApiKey header. API keys are generated from nuget.org account settings and can be scoped to specific packages or glob patterns, with configurable expiry. This is better than most registries — package-scoped keys are supported.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Fully free for all public package reads and publishes
pricing.paid_tiers: None for NuGet.org. Azure Artifacts (private feeds) is a separate paid service.
pricing.notes: NuGet.org is a free Microsoft-operated service. Publishing public packages is free. Rate limits exist to protect the service but are not published in detail.

performance.rate_limits: No hard published rate limits. The service index and package metadata endpoints are highly cacheable (CDN-backed). Package downloads served via Azure CDN.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error responses are JSON with a structured errors array containing message strings. HTTP status codes are used correctly. Rate limit responses include appropriate headers. Documentation on error conditions is better than most registries but still incomplete in edge cases.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: GET operations are fully idempotent and heavily cacheable. Package push is not idempotent — pushing the same version twice returns a 409 conflict. Package deletion (unlist) is idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: The v3 API begins with a service index (https://api.nuget.org/v3/index.json) that must be fetched first to discover all endpoint URLs — agents cannot hardcode endpoint paths
Package IDs are case-insensitive but canonical casing is preserved in metadata — store the canonical ID, not the user-input casing
NuGet package versions follow SemVer but with NuGet-specific extensions (e.g., pre-release labels) that differ from strict SemVer2
Package deletion on NuGet.org is actually unlisting (soft delete) — packages remain downloadable by direct URL but are hidden from search
API keys can be scoped to specific package IDs via glob patterns — agents should use the minimum-scope key for publish workflows
The search endpoint returns `totalHits` which may exceed actual paginated results due to Lucene index behavior
Package symbols (.snupkg) require a separate push to the symbols server endpoint

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API keys support package-scoped permissions (a significant improvement over other registries) and configurable expiry dates. The X-NuGet-ApiKey header is non-standard. No OAuth flow. NuGet.org performs basic malware scanning on uploaded packages. Package signing (Authenticode/NuGet signatures) is supported but not enforced for community packages.

score_rationale: The NuGet v3 API is one of the better-designed package registry APIs, with a service index discovery pattern, good documentation, and package-scoped API keys. It loses points for lacking an MCP server, having no published rate limits, and the service-index-first pattern requiring an extra round trip. The documentation is solid by registry standards. Well-suited for .NET dependency analysis and automated publish workflows with appropriate key scoping.
